item.title: Adjacent Fractions
item.description: We look at all of the rational numbers in $ (0, 1] $ whose denominator is smaller than some $ n \in \mathbb N $. Prove that for any two adjacent numbers $ \frac {p_i} {q_i}, \frac {p_{i+1}} {q_{i+1}} $ the following holds: $ p_{i+1} q_i - p_i q_{i+1} = 1 $. Here’s an example for $ n = 4 $: Here’s another (with the same code) for $ n = 7 $:

item.title: Segment Splitting
item.description:
Given a set of pairwise-disjoint intervals (such that the intersection of every pair is $ \emptyset $) in $ [0, 1] $, assuming the total length is greater than $ \frac 1 2 $, show there are two points $ a,b $ in the union of the segments such that $ |a-b| = \frac 1 {10} $. Here are a few examples
from manim import * class SegmentSplittingExample(Scene): min_width = .

item.title: Triangle Areas
item.description: Given a group $ S $ of points $ (x,y) \in \mathbb R ^ 2 $ such that every three points in $ S $ form a triangle (aren’t collinear) of area at most $ 1 $, show that there is some triangle with area at most 2 which bounds $ S $. Solution The idea is to look at the three points which make the triangle with the largest area, and note the following observation:

item.title: Grasshopping
item.description: A grasshopper sits in a point $ M \in \mathbb R ^ 2 $, outside of the square $ [0, 1] ^ 2 $. Each time, they face towards the origin and choose the rightmost corner of the square they are able to see (from their perspective). Then they go to the symmetric point with regard to it. That’s quite a difficult explanation of their moves, so here’s one without words - an example of the first 50 hops when the grasshopper starts at $ (3, 2) $:

item.title: Ant Meeting
item.description: Assume you have $ n $ ants located at some points $ x_n $ along the number line. At each step, two of the $ n $ ants are chosen to meet each other and they both travel to the middle between them, e.g. $ x_1, x_2 \longrightarrow \frac{x_1 + x_2}{2}, \frac{x_1 + x_2}{2} $. You may even take $ \infty $ steps. What is the maximum total distance which can be achieved?

item.title: Stacking Game
item.description: You have $ n $ boxes organized in a circle, where each box contains a ball. You are only allowed to move two balls to adjacent boxes in opposite directions (clockwise and counter-clockwise). Given $ n $, determine if you can move all the balls to a single box. Solution Let us take a closer look at $ n = 2 $. Sadly, we are stuck! How about $ n = 3 $?

item.title: Matrix Reordering
item.description: Given a matrix of size $ m \times n $ which contains numbers from $ 1 $ to $ n $ and each number appears exactly $ m $ times, show that one can reorder the numbers inside each column such that the resulting matrix would have the numbers $ 1 $ to $ n $ in every line. First, a (few) quick demo(s) to understand: These demos all share a single codebase which creates an animation given the input.

item.title: Square Covering
item.description: An odd number of $ n \times n $ squares is placed on the integer points of an infinite grid, such that any two may overlap. Show that there are at least $ n^2 $ small $ 1 \times 1 $ grid squares that are covered by an odd number of squares. First, let’s create an animations showing this for $ n = 3 $: The code looks like so:

item.title: Square Covering Variations
item.description: This page contains variations of the square covering parity riddle. You should read it before continuing. Rectangles There are a few things that the proof did not require (hence we can generalize it). For example, we never really used the fact that we are describing square. The riddle can be easily described for rectangles: An odd number of $ m \times n $ rectangles is placed on an infinite grid, such that any two may overlap.
